summary:
The Eastern Zhou dynasty (770–256 BC) was the second and longer phase of Zhou rule in China, beginning after the [[event:2002|Sack of Haojing]] forced the court's relocation to Luoyi (Luoyang) and ending when the last Zhou king surrendered to the expanding Qin state. The Eastern Zhou is subdivided into the Spring and Autumn Period (770–476 BC) and the Warring States Period (475–221 BC), representing a progressive intensification of interstate competition and political fragmentation before Qin unification.

Despite the Zhou kings' political impotence — they retained ritual authority but commanded no military force and controlled only a tiny territory — the Eastern Zhou period was culturally extraordinary. The philosophical flowering of the [[event:1112|Hundred Schools of Thought]], including [[event:1954|Confucius]], [[event:1955|Laozi and Daoism Origins]], and many other philosophical schools, occurred in this period of political crisis. Advances in iron technology, agricultural productivity, and urban growth created the economic foundation for the population growth and state competition of the Warring States. The Zhou court served as a model of ritual propriety that all the competing states claimed to follow while ignoring in practice, and Zhou cultural traditions — including the written language, ritual music, and canonical texts — provided the shared cultural infrastructure that gave Chinese civilization its coherence even as it was politically fragmented.
